coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection and determination of benzodiazepines using silver nanoparticles-N-GQDs ink modified electrode: A new platform for modern pharmaceutical analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Ashrafi, Hamideh
Hassanpour, Soodabeh
Saadati, Arezoo
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Ansarin, Khalil
Ozkan, Sibel Ayşil
Shadjou, Nasrin
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a unique conductive nano-ink based on silver nanoparticle‑nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Ag/N-GQD) was synthesized and utilized for development of a novel, unique and sensitive biosensor for determination of alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam. Electrochemical deposition, as a well-controlled synthesis procedure, has been utilized for subsequently layer-by-layer preparation of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink on a chitosan (CS) electropolymerized on the surface of gold electrode using cyclic voltammetry techniques in the regime of −1 to +1 V. The modified electrode appeared as an efficient electroactive interface for detection of selected benzodiazepines by using cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, and square wave voltammetry. The prepared sensor showed a noticeably good electro-activity for electrooxidation of alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam than bare CS and Ag/N-GQD modified electrodes. Enhancement of peak currents is ascribed to the fast heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics that arise from the synergistic coupling between unique features of CS such as high permeability, excellent film-forming capability, good adhesion, cheapness, nontoxicity, and a susceptibility to chemical modifications, Ag/N-GQD nano-ink as high electrical conductivity. Finally, application of the new sensor for determination of selected benzodiazepine group drugs in unprocessed human plasma samples was studied. In general, the simultaneous attachment of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink to structure of CS provides new opportunities toward biomedical analysis and the personal healthcare.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomedical science
Conductive ink
Pharmaceutical analysis
Electropolymerization
Benzodiazepine
Drug
Silver nanoparticle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and conductivity study of ink
Silver nanoparticles deposited on the N-GQD
Electropolymerization of CS supported Ag/N-GQD nano-ink
Comparison the performance of prepared electrodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number [971242] from the National Institutes for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. Also, we thank Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research. This study was partially supported by Tuberculosis and Lung Disease Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences as PhD thesis of Hamideh Ashrafi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographic images of two different patterns obtained by Ag/N-GQD nano-ink on photo paper.

(A) Electropolymerization of CS (0.002 g CS dispersed in the 0.1 M HCl acid) on the surface of Au electrode using repetitive CV. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Number of cycle is 20. (B) Electrochemical deposition of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink on CS-modified electrode using repetitive CV. Scan rate is 50 mV/s. Number of cycle is 30.

(A) FE-SEM images of the bulk Ag/N-GQD nano-ink, (B) Ag/N-GQD nano-ink deposited on Au electrode, (C) CS electropolymerized on the surface of Au electrode, (D) CS-Ag/N-GQD coated on the surface of Au electrode.
The schematic illustration of the CS-Ag/N-GQD nano-ink modified gold electrode preparation.
Kinetic and analytical figure of merit.

row.entry: Linear range for I (μA) versus v (V/s)

Slop of ln Ip (μA)
versus ln v (V/s)

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection and determination of benzodiazepines using silver nanoparticles-N-GQDs ink modified electrode: A new platform for modern pharmaceutical analysis

Correspondence to: A. Jouyban, Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a unique conductive nano-ink based on silver nanoparticle‑nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Ag/N-GQD) was synthesized and utilized for development of a novel, unique and sensitive biosensor for determination of alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam. Electrochemical deposition, as a well-controlled synthesis procedure, has been utilized for subsequently layer-by-layer preparation of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink on a chitosan (CS) electropolymerized on the surface of gold electrode using cyclic voltammetry techniques in the regime of −1 to +1 V. The modified electrode appeared as an efficient electroactive interface for detection of selected benzodiazepines by using cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, and square wave voltammetry. The prepared sensor showed a noticeably good electro-activity for electrooxidation of alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam than bare CS and Ag/N-GQD modified electrodes. Enhancement of peak currents is ascribed to the fast heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics that arise from the synergistic coupling between unique features of CS such as high permeability, excellent film-forming capability, good adhesion, cheapness, nontoxicity, and a susceptibility to chemical modifications, Ag/N-GQD nano-ink as high electrical conductivity. Finally, application of the new sensor for determination of selected benzodiazepine group drugs in unprocessed human plasma samples was studied. In general, the simultaneous attachment of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink to structure of CS provides new opportunities toward biomedical analysis and the personal healthcare.

ce:section.ce:para: Conductive inks have different types such as metal inks, nanoparticle inks, and conductive polymers. In the nanoparticle based inks, nanoparticles come in suspension in the organic solvent (including toluene, ethylene glycol, and etc.) or water. The utilized solvent should be easily evaporated after deposition, but not so fast that it is dried in a short time and becomes a viscose film and prevents the expulsion of the drop [1]. To make sure proper flow, the particles should be uniformly distributed in solution. Although the viscosity of these inks increases, based on accumulation of particles in the suspension, it can be used to specific polymeric coatings on the surface of the nanoparticles to prevent accumulation. Using these polymer coatings, it is possible to ensure the dispersion and stability of the nanoparticles. Considering the possibility of producing large amounts of nanoparticles and also due to the ability to disperse in great concentrations and create proper electrical conductivity, nano-inks have extensive applications [1].

For the production of conductive inks, metals such as silver [2,3], gold [4] and conductive polymers have been investigated [5]. Despite the remarkable benefits, these materials have disadvantages include a high price of gold and silver when applied in great quantities. Conductive polymers have limited application due to low chemical and thermal resistance and comparatively little conductivity, but are beneficial for flexible displays. The application of carbon nanotubes to produce conductive inks is also limited by their poor dispersion [6,7]. Therefore, it is necessary to study the better compounds for the preparation of conductive inks.

Recently, graphene-based inks have been applied for construction of bio-devices and printable elastic electronics due to its great mechanical, chemical stability and its unique and distinctive electronic features. Because of extremely low solubility of graphene in water-based inks and inclination of graphene nanosheets to aggregate by van der Waals interactions and tough π-π stacking disposition, the pre-printed soluble GO (graphene oxide) ink is reduced at 400 °C for obtaining printed conductive patterns based on graphene [8,9]. Its attractive characteristics combined with solution-processing capability and the ease of preparation, indicate that low-cost, flexible inkjet printer graphene-based technique may have great potential applications in detectors, electronic devices and electronic circuits [10]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has become widely used in the development of electrochemical sensors due to its significant advantages, such as high electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, broad and inexpensive resource, water solubility, chemical inactivity, and poor toxicity [11]. The chemical and electronic properties of GQDs have been altered as a result of doping with nitrogen and enhance their catalytic activity for redox reactions [12]. N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are optimal crystals with a diameter of 2.5 to 8.5 nm [13]. N-GQDs is synthesized using a variety of methods including: chemical solution, organic, electrochemical, acid oxidation [13,14], solvothermal [15] and hydrothermal [16,17], the latter is widely used because of the average reaction conditions, the simple procedure and the use of relatively inexpensive devices. Synchronous, the use of low-cost environmental resources, such as amino acids, is highly regarded as a source of nitrogen for the synthesis of N-GQDs rather than organic compound and expensive ion liquids [14,18,19]. The prominent physicochemical properties of N-GQDs have led to the development of its application in various fields, including photocatalysts, electrodes and sensors. The uniform size, high quantum yields, low toxicity and biocompatibility of N-GQDs will doubtlessly be used in more attractive applications [20]. Because of expensive and take more time of conventional methods for pharmaceutical analysis, biosensors are an appropriate alternative for the analysis of drugs that have been developed due to their simplicity, selectivity, sensitivity, speed and low cost [21].

Benzodiazepines, such as clonazepam, diazepam, alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide, oxazepam, and etc. act on the brain and affect memory, sentimental reactions, thought, co-ordination, and muscle modulation [22]. Diazepam (DZP) (7-chloro-1-methyl-5-phenyl-3H-1,4-benzodiazepin-2(1H)-one) is broadly used to treatment of muscle spasms, insomnia, anxiety, and epilepsy [23,24]. This drug affects the brain and nerves by transferring the sedation effect using γ-aminobutyric acid (a chemical neurotransmitter that causes the nerves to communicate with each other) [25]. Oxazepam (OZP) (7-chloro-1,3-dihydro-3-hydroxy-5-phenyl-2H-1,4-benzod-iapin-2-one) is used in aggressive and delusional patients. Oxazepam is derived from the metabolism of diazepam in the body and is excreted in the form of glucuronide conjugated via urine and bile [26]. Alprazolam is another group of benzodiazepines and a short-acting psychoactive drug with high efficacy. The prescription of this drug is oral and it used as an antidepressant, anti-anxiety, hypnotic, tranquilizer, and anti-epileptic drug. Muscle numbness, acute drowsiness, co-ordination problems, fainting and deadly can be due to an overdose of alprazolam. Therefore, the detection of these drugs is important for legal and clinical toxicologists [27]. Clonazepam (5-(2-chlorphenyl)-1,3-dihydro-7-nitro-2H-1,4-benzodiazepin-2-one) is one of the benzodiazepine derivatives, which is used as an anticonvulsant drug for the treatment of epilepsy in children and adults. It is administered as an oral dose to prevent a convulsion to be injected intravenously in epileptic conditions and neonatal seizures. Chlordiazepoxide (CDP) (7-chloro-N-methyl-5-phenyl-3H-1,4-benzodiazepine-2-amino-4-oxide) is used to relieve tension, anxiety and control of agitation related with acute alcohol removal. Therefore, in order to control the dose of clonazepam in the plasma of patients, monitoring of its concentration is of particular importance [28,29].

Whereas preservation of the severe serum level of these drugs affects the treatment with them, the using up benzodiazepines may lead to killing accidents. Therefore, benzodiazepine analysis is important in many areas, including legal research, quality control, and clinical studies [21].

According to advantages of nanomaterial based inks on the construction of bio-devices for monitoring of drug, role of analytical methods on the determination of benzodiazepines, in the present study, a novel electrochemical sensor based on Ag/N-GQD conductive nano-ink was developed for the determination of benzodiazepines including clonazepam, diazepam, chlordiazepoxide bis, oxazepam, and alprazolam in pharmaceutical and human plasma samples. To prepare the desired biosensor, the Ag/N-GQD nano-ink was first synthesized and then electrodeposited on the surface of the poly chitosan modified gold electrode. Analytical function of the sensor was applied to measure benzodiazepines by means of differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV). To the best acknowledge, this is the first report on the application of nano-inks for the analysis of benzodiazepines which is necessary in quality control of drugs and clinical laboratories.

All chemicals applied in this research were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Ontario, Canada) and had analytical reagent grade. All drugs were purchased from Sobhan Daru Co. (Iran). All solutions were prepared by using double-distilled water. The stock solution of each drug was prepared through dissolving an exact mass of bulk drugs in a suitable volume of 0.1 M sodium hydroxide solution (NaOH), which was utilized also as supporting electrolyte, and kept in the dark place at 4 °C. Further dilute solutions were prepared daily by exact dilution just before usage. Human plasma samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran).

Electrochemical measurements were accomplished in an ordinary three-electrode cell (from Metrohm) powered by an electrochemical system composed of PalmSense 4c system, electrochemical interface. The system was run on a laptop using PSTrace5 software. The ac voltage amplitude used was 10 mV and the equilibrium time was 2 s. Ag/AgCl-Sat'd KCl (from Metrohm) and a platinum wire and were utilized as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was a gold electrode (d = 2 mm) (from Azar Electrode Co., Urumia, Iran). For DPV (differential-pulse voltammetry) measurements step potential = 0.2 V, Epulse = 0.05 V, tpulse = 0.2 s and a scan rate = 50 mVs−1, and for SWV (square wave voltammetry) measurements a frequency = 10.0 Hz, pulse amplitude = 0.02 V and Epulse = 0.05 V were employed. The surface morphology of electrode was characterized by FE-SEM (high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope, Hitachi-SU8020, Czech) with an operating voltage of 3 kV, and chemical compositions of the electrode were analyzed by an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with the FE-SEM equipment. Conductivity of nano-ink was examined by EcoScan CON 6 Palmtop Conductivity Meter with electrode (EC-CONSEN91B). Also, the resistance of conductive nano-ink was evaluated by ohmmeter (XIOLE, XL830L, China, Multimeter).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and conductivity study of ink
Silver nanoparticles deposited on the N-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQD was synthesized and characterized by our research group according to pervious work [30]. Then, 0.4 g PVP K-30 and 0.6 g graphite powder were added into 2 mL of N-GQD solution, which was heated in water bath at 60 °C. The mixture was magnetically stirred until complete dissolving of PVP and graphite powder. Then, 600 μL aqueous solution of AgNO3 (0.2 M) was directly added into above solution and 5 min magnetically stirred in order to guarantee efficient blending of N-GQD and Ag+. Subsequently, the mixed solution maintained 12 h at 60 °C in incubator without agitation. After 12 h the solution was heated at 80 °C and magnetically stirred again. At last, 400 μL of NaOH (sodium hydroxide, 8 M) solution was drop wisely added in order to increase the reduction impact. The product centrifuged for 30 min at 8000 rpm and 3 times washed with distilled water (DI) for removing extra reactant. In order to produce hybrid conductive nano-ink, Ag/N-GQD nano-ink was dispersed into ethanol, DI water, ethylene glycol (EG) with volume ratio of 11.25:11.25:2.5 sonicating for 30 min that resulted in production of water-based ink.

For this purpose, different conductive patterns designed on the photographic sheets surface by Ag/N-GQD composite. As illustrated in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1 (see Supporting Information), different types of conducting schemas (e.g., spiral and TBZMED writing) and five 1.5 V battery and a dip LED with a rated voltage of 3.0 V was fabricated on paper substrate. One of the legs of LED (cathode) was connected to battery anode and so cathode surface of battery fixed to one side of conductive line through a thine wire. Once the anode leg of LED touched to other side of conductive line, the electronic circuit switched on and the LED was lighted up. Afterwards, the resistance of conductive lines was measured by ohmmeter, 113.7 kΩ and 85.2 kΩ were obtained for spiral and TBZMED schemes, respectively. The conductivity of produced nano-ink was 384 μs. To demonstrated reliability and flexibility of conductive paths, it bent outwards by 90°.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electropolymerization of CS supported Ag/N-GQD nano-ink
ce:section.ce:para: Before experiments, gold (Au) electrode was polished with 0.05 μm alumina (Beuhler, USA) slurries. Then, the electrode was ultrasonically rinsed successively with 1:1 absolute alcohol, nitric acid and DI water, and allowed to dry at room temperature. At the first step of preparing modified electrode, a three electrode system was transferred into 0.1 M HCl containing 0.002 g chitosan (CS), and 20 repetitive cycles in the potential range from −1 V to +1 V versus Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 100 mV/s (Fig. 2 A). Since, CS dissolved in HCl acid is positively charged, so the hydrophilic CS can interact via OH side groups with Au electrode to form a polymeric film on the electrode surface [31] which enhance the adhesion of nano-ink to the surface. Subsequently, the CS modified Au electrode was put into the nano-ink suspension, and swept for 30 segments at the scan rate of 50 mV/s in the potential range from −1 V to +1 V (Fig. 2B). As can be seen, during the polymerization process, indiscernible peaks started to appear after second peak. An anodic peak at 0.7 V and a reduction peak at 0.2 V were observed due to the formation of Ag/N-GQD film. The peak current increased with the enhancement of cyclic voltammetric scans number showing that an electroconductive film was formed on the CS modified Au electrode surface. Lastly, the oxidation peak disappeared after complete formation of Ag/N-GQD film. These results indicate that the hydrophilic CS can interact with N-GQDs via NH2 and COOH side groups, which enable forming distinctive network between the chitosan chains. Finally, conductive and stable film of CS-Ag/N-GQD was obtained at the surface of Au electrode (see Scheme 1 ).

To evaluate the surface morphology of bulk Ag/N-GQD nano-ink, Ag/N-GQD electrodeposited on Au electrode, CS electrodeposited on Au electrode and CS-Ag/NGQD electrodeposited on Au electrode, it was examined by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM). FE-SEM images of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink, Ag/NGQD-Au electrode, CS/Au electrode, CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode are demonstrated in Fig. 3 . As can be seen, in the bulk Ag/N-GQD nano-ink, the small sized Ag nanoparticles with spherical uniform structure decorated on the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD). It is interesting to observe that Ag/N-GQD nano-ink prepared in water/ethanol/EG demonstrates no aggregation of nanoparticles. In following of the standard colloids theory, the colloid stability was controlled by the balance between charged particles Coulombic repulsion and van der Waals interaction [32]. It is significant to mention that PVP graphitic Ag/N-GQD nano-ink has a remarkable effect on conductivity alteration due to anchoring of Ag NPs onto N-GQDs. The FE-SEM images of Ag/N-GQD electrodeposited directly on the surface of Au electrode in the absence of other materials exhibited a regular and patterned structure. Significantly, CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode demonstrates a compact film with distribution of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink on the surface of CS which can be helpful in entrapments of target analytes. Consequently, these results confirmed that Au electrode was successfully coated by CS-Ag/N-GQD film, resulting in alteration in the surface activity of electrode. Also, these results confirm that dispersion of CS on the surface of electrode was performed successfully. CS is dissolved in hydrochloric acid (HCl), thus forming a regular polymer film.

As evident in Fig. S2, signals for C and N elements belong to both Ag/N-GQD and CS, while Ag is only attributed to Ag-N-GQD and O is only attributed to chitosan. The large and sharp C and N peaks are the typical signals for bulk carbon and nitrogen elements in the Ag/N-GQD nano-ink (Fig. S2A (see Supporting Information)). Another reason for large and sharp C peak is related to existence of graphite in the nano-ink formation through van der Waals bond (CC) also, AgNO3 bond to the carboxyl group of N-GQD. The corresponding EDS spectra of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink electrodeposited on the surface of gold electrode and CS-Ag/N-GQD/Au electrode (Fig. S2B & C (see supporting information)) demonstrate the increment of Ag peak intensity, which show the deposition of nano-size silver layer along with N-GQD. Also, they indicate that C, N and O elements in two electrode overlapped with each other in binding to the surface of Au electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison the performance of prepared electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behaviors of different modification steps were analyzed by measuring the CVs at 0.1 M NaOH (as supporting electrolyte) (Fig. S3A (see supporting information)). Fig. S3 (see supporting information) compare the CV response of CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode with Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode and CS-Au electrode in the presence of selected drugs. The CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode displays well-fined oxidation peaks for all selected analytes (Alprazolam, Clonazepam, Diazepam, oxazepam, chlordiazepoxide bis) than others.

The CVs of Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode, CS-Au electrode and CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode were recorded between −1 V and +1 V, the scan rate of 100 mV/s in the presence of alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam. As seen in Fig. S3 (see supporting information), on the Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode, CS-Au electrode, and CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode one redox peak was appeared. Also, using CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode the oxidation peaks were appeared in 0.5002, 0.5002, 0.5002, 0.5002 and 0.6003 V, respectively for alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam with difference in current. These peaks attribute to the anodic oxidation of selected drugs using modified electrodes. This observation is also attributed to the inherent ability and high conductivity of modifiers. Also, the anodic peak current at the surface of CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode is lower than CS-Au electrode, and Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode, while the cathodic peak current is approximately in the same level. It can be concluded that after insertion of CS and Ag/N-GQD used for modification of Au electrode, there is not high acceleration in the current of oxidation of drugs in comparison to two other modified electrodes. But it has better electrocatalytic/electroactive behavior for redox reaction of alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam with completely discernible anodic peak which demonstrated the electrochemical recognition following electron transfer with selected drugs. This observation is also linked to the impressive electrocatalytic activity and high conductivity of Ag/N-GQDs deposited on the surface of CS modified Au electrode. It is no doubt that CS-Ag/N-GQDs play important role in electroactivity improvement of Au electrode toward electrooxidation of five selected drugs. CS-Ag/N-GQDs film provide catalytically platform for sensitive detection of drugs; meanwhile, Ag/N-GQDs can amplify the electrochemical signals produced during the electrochemical sensing process. Also, CS provide a stable surface for Ag/N-GQDs. In one word, the synergetic effect of Ag/N-GQDs and CS lead to a successful determination and detection of alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam.

Scan rate is one of significant parameters affecting electroactivity of various organic molecules. The electrooxidation of selected drugs at different scan rates was evaluated on the surface of CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode by CV technique (Fig. S4 (see Supporting Information). A linear relationship was obtained between peak current and square root of scan rate which revealed that the oxidation of selected drugs was adsorption-controlled step (Fig. S5C (see Supporting Information)). Two approaches broadly utilized to study the reversibility of reactions and to determine whether a reaction is diffusion or adsorption controlled consist of the analysis of dependences: I
p versus v
1/2 and Ln I
p versus Ln v. Figs. S5(B and C (see supporting information)),) demonstrate these plots for the oxidation peak of selected drugs in 0.1 M NaOH. For irreversible or reversible systems without kinetic complications, I
p varies linearly with v 1/2, interception the origin. Though, the plot of I
p on v 1/2 exhibited in Fig. S5C (see supporting information), is linear (average R2 = 0.98328), it crosses the origin of axes. In the selected scan rate range from 2 to 1000 mV/s, oxidation peak currents (I
p ) of selected drugs electrooxidation depends linearly on square root of the scan rate (V) and is described by the following equations:

This dependence does not cross the origin (Fig. S5C (see Supporting Information)). This fact can suggest that the electrode process isn't controlled by diffusion and can be preceded by chemical reaction. On the other hand, a dependence of ln Ipa on ln v is linear (Fig. S5B (see Supporting Information)) and described by the following equations:
Its slope is 1.50 (average slope) and indicates adsorption-control of the electrode process. A slope close to 1 is expected for adsorption-controlled electrode processes [33–37].

If electrochemical reaction is irreversible, then Epa is independent on v. Thus, it can be concluded that heterogeneous electron transfers in clonazepam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, alprazolam, and oxazepam electrooxidation is irreversible because Epa increases with an increase in the scan rate [38–40].

As highly sensitive electrochemical methods, the DPV (differential pulse voltammetry) and SWV (square wave voltammetry) have much higher current sensitivity, lower background current and better resolution in comparison to CV. According to the achieved results (Figs. S6 & S7 (see supporting information)), it was possible to apply these methods to the quantitative analysis of selected drugs. As mentioned previously, the 0.1 M NaOH was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of drugs. The linear range was evaluated under optimum conditions employing DPV and SWV techniques. The analytical parameters for the detection of selected drugs using CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode have been evaluated by measuring the DPVs and SWVs of selected analytes with different concentrations (Figs. S6A & S7A (see supporting information)). A plot between selected drugs concentration and peak currents (Figs. S6B & S7B (see supporting information)) indicates a linear relationship was approved. All of the analytical results were summarized in Table 1 . The results show that the CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode holds good electroactivity to the oxidation of selected drugs which may because of synergic effect of CS and Ag/N-GQDs to providing abundant active site for this purpose. This leads to sensitive detection and determination of benzodiazepines.

Stability and reproducibility are two significant factors that are studied to investigate the performance of the biosensor. Electrode stability related to the number of cycles was checked. The results indicate that with increase of the cycle's numbers to 100, the peak currents increase very smoothly. Results confirmed that the electrode is stable at least 100 replicate analysis (Fig. S8 A (see Supporting Information)). To check the inter-electrode reproducibility of modified electrode with CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode, three modified electrodes were tested at the same time by recording DPV in 0.1 M NaOH supporting electrolyte (Fig. S8 (see Supporting Information)). Also, the stability of the CS-Ag/N-GQD-Au electrode in the terms of peak current was studied by storing the electrode in dark condition for 6 days and recording DPVs. The voltammetric behavior of the proposed electrochemical sensor in 0.1 M NaOH supporting electrolyte demonstrate that the electrode was stable until 4 days by smooth increase and by the fifth day peak current decreased and the signal was not retrained (Fig. S8 C (see supporting information)).

According to the achieved results, it was possible to apply DPV technique to the quantitative analysis of selected drugs in real samples. As mentioned before, 0.1 M NaOH was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of selected analytes. For this purpose, 20 μL of unprocessed human plasma were mixed with 2 mL of different concentrations of selected drugs. The linear range for determination of selected drugs in unprocessed human plasma was investigated under optimal conditions. Also, the calibration plot was obtained the peak current against the mixture of selected drugs with plasma concentrations (Fig. S9 (see Supporting Information). The linear range for all of selected drugs and lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) were mentioned in Table 1.

In summary, we report for the first time the synthesis of a novel conductive Ag/N-GQD nano-ink for efficient recognition and quantification of alprazolam, chlordiazepoxide bis, diazepam, oxazepam and clonazepam. The polymeric conductive film composed of CS and Ag/N-GQD which were successfully electrodeposited on the surface of gold electrode using layer by layer strategy. In contrast to bare CS-modified and Ag/N-GQD-modified gold electrode, the CS-Ag/N-GQDs film combines the advantages of CS, as a biocompatible agent lead to better adhesion of Ag/N-GQD to the surface of electrode and Ag/N-GQD with excellent electrical conductivity, and thus, it is employed successfully to providing excellent biocompatible microenvironment and plentiful active sites for electrooxidation and sensitive detection of chlordiazepoxide bis, alprazolam, clonazepam, diazepam, and oxazepam in the standard and plasma samples. More importantly, the proposed conductive nano-ink-based sensor was employed for determination of selected benzodiazepines without the necessity for sample pretreatment or any time-consuming extraction or evaporation steps prior to the analysis with satisfactory analytical results. It is expected that this biocompatible nano-ink-based sensor will provide an inexpensive, eco-friendly sensitive, fast electrochemical sensing. Finally, the results indicate that CS has essential role in the signal amplification of Ag/N-GQD which is important in electroanalytical chemistry. Therefore, the attachment of CS to structure of Ag/N-GQD nano-ink opens new windows for the personal healthcare and modern pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number [971242] from the National Institutes for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. Also, we thank Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research. This study was partially supported by Tuberculosis and Lung Disease Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences as PhD thesis of Hamideh Ashrafi.
